sub_considerations.one_liner: Decide the one thing this article should accomplish before opening the editor.
sub_considerations.body:
Articles that try to inform, persuade and convert in equal measure usually do none of them well. Pick the primary user task — read for understanding, follow a how-to, evaluate a position — and let it shape every other decision: title length, lead structure, where (if anywhere) a CTA sits.

If you cannot finish the sentence "after reading this, the user should be able to ___" in fewer than 12 words, the brief isn't done yet.
sub_considerations.source_title: How users read on the web

sub_considerations.one_liner: Map the article to a single dominant search intent — informational, navigational, transactional, or commercial.
sub_considerations.body:
Search engines and increasingly LLM-based answer engines route queries to pages by best-matching intent. A page that mixes intents (e.g. a how-to with three paragraphs of brand history before the steps) competes for nothing well.

Write a one-line intent statement next to the working title. If the body drifts, either fix the body or fork the article.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Aim for a reading level one or two grades below your audience's expected literacy ceiling.
sub_considerations.body:
WCAG SC 3.1.5 (Reading Level, AAA) suggests that when text requires reading ability more advanced than lower-secondary, a simpler version or supplemental content should be available. In practice: short sentences, common words, glossed jargon.

Hemingway, Datayze, or built-in word processor tools all approximate this. Don't tune for the score — tune for the reader.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Lead with the angle in the brief, not the topic. "Why X is overrated" beats "Notes on X".
sub_considerations.body:
A topic ("typography on mobile") is the room; an angle ("variable fonts make the case for fewer breakpoints") is what you do in it. The brief should commit to the angle before a single paragraph is drafted.

Editors test this with the "and so?" question. If the angle survives three of them, ship the brief.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Length follows argument, not the other way around. Don't pad to hit a target word count.
sub_considerations.body:
The "1,500 words for SEO" rule of thumb is a misreading of correlation studies. Search engines reward articles that satisfy intent fully; an intent satisfied in 400 words doesn't get more satisfied by 1,100 more words.

Inverse rule: if your draft is short, ask whether you've over-trimmed structure. If it's long, ask whether you've under-edited argument.
sub_considerations.source_title: Long vs short articles as content strategy

sub_considerations.one_liner: Pin tone of voice to two or three adjectives in the brief, with one anti-adjective for each.
sub_considerations.body:
"Confident but not arrogant; clear but not patronising; warm but not cute." The anti-adjective is the work — without it, "confident" drifts to "shouty" without anyone noticing.

If two writers in the team disagree on whether a paragraph fits, the brief's anti-adjectives are usually the missing referee.
sub_considerations.source_title: Content design: planning, writing and managing content

sub_considerations.one_liner: Use lowercase kebab-case. Hyphens separate words; underscores do not.
sub_considerations.body:
Google's URL structure guide explicitly recommends hyphens over underscores for word boundaries. Underscores are treated as word-joiners; type_inference is one token, type-inference is two.

Capitals work but introduce case-sensitivity surprises on some servers and in user expectations. Lowercase everything.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Keep dates out of the URL unless the article is genuinely time-bound (e.g. an annual report).
sub_considerations.body:
A URL like /2021/05/typography-on-mobile/ ages the article by definition. When you update it in 2026, you either lie via the URL or break every existing link.

Flat slugs (/typography-on-mobile/) are durable. Use datePublished and dateModified in structured data for the date facts.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Keep slugs under ~60 characters; truncate the working title, don't extend it.
sub_considerations.body:
Long URLs get truncated in shared previews and look untrustworthy. Strip stop words ("a", "the", "and", "of") from the slug; the title element can keep them.

If you can drop a word from the slug and a reader who pasted only the URL still understands the topic, drop it.

sub_considerations.one_liner: When you change a slug, ship a 301 from the old URL. Always. No exceptions.
sub_considerations.body:
A 301 transfers PageRank, signals permanence to crawlers, and preserves backlinks. 302s don't — they say "we'll be back". Use 302 only for genuine temporary redirects (A/B tests, geo-routing).

Keep a redirect map under version control. Stale redirects pile up; review once a year.

RewriteEngine On
Redirect 301 /old-slug /new-slug

sub_considerations.one_liner: Use the eyebrow for section or topic, not as a second headline.
sub_considerations.body:
The eyebrow ("Opinion", "Tutorial", "Long read", "Performance") tells the reader what kind of article they're about to read. It's a frame, not content. Two to four words. ALL CAPS or letterspaced small caps both work.

Semantically the eyebrow is part of the heading group. Use a <p> with role context, or wrap title and eyebrow in <hgroup> (now well-supported in WHATWG HTML).

sub_considerations.one_liner: If the eyebrow only repeats the section name from the breadcrumb, drop it; visual hierarchy beats redundancy.
sub_considerations.body: Screen reader users get the breadcrumb and the eyebrow back-to-back; two pieces of the same information feels like a stutter. Audit content so eyebrow earns its row.

sub_considerations.one_liner: WCAG 2.4.6 requires headings and labels to describe topic or purpose.
sub_considerations.body:
SC 2.4.6 (AA) is the rule against clever, opaque headings. Screen reader users navigate by heading; cute titles that hide the topic ("Down the rabbit hole") force a full read just to know what the page is.

Article H1: state the subject. Reserve cleverness for the lead.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Match the H1 to the <title> element unless SEO demands a longer title.
sub_considerations.body:
The on-page H1 and the document <title> are usually the same. When they diverge, the title carries the search-engine load — site name suffix, secondary keyword — and the H1 carries the editorial load.

On mobile. Browser tabs truncate at ~25 characters. Front-load the most distinctive word.

On desktop. Titles up to ~60 characters render in full in Google's SERP. Suffix style: Title — Site name.

sub_considerations.one_liner: One H1 per page. Google has tolerated multiple H1s since 2019; that doesn't make it a good idea.
sub_considerations.body: Assistive tech still uses the document outline to convey "what page is this". Multiple H1s flatten it. The HTML5 outline algorithm that promised <section>-scoped H1s never shipped in browsers or screen readers — write H1–H6 manually.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Aim for ~50–70 characters in the H1. Long enough to be specific, short enough to scan.
sub_considerations.body:
Headings that exceed two display lines stop being scannable. Use text-wrap: balance; to keep the break point graceful at narrow widths.

h1 { text-wrap: balance; }
sub_considerations.source_title: Microcontent: a few words go a long way

sub_considerations.one_liner: Front-load distinctive words. The reader's eye and the SERP both lose attention left-to-right.
sub_considerations.body: Eye-tracking work going back to the early F-pattern studies shows that the first two words of a heading do disproportionate work. "An introduction to variable fonts" buries the lede; "Variable fonts: an introduction" earns the click.

sub_considerations.one_liner: The lead's job is to make the reader continue. Don't summarize the article — promise it.
sub_considerations.body: Standfirsts that summarize give the reader permission to leave. Standfirsts that tease commit to a payoff the body has to deliver. Bias toward tease, then make the body keep the promise.

sub_considerations.one_liner: One or two sentences, around 25–40 words. Set in a larger size than body copy.
sub_considerations.body: A larger size (1.2×–1.4× body) and slightly tighter line height visually distinguish the lead from the body. Resist the urge to colour it; weight and size carry enough.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Don't mark the lead up as an H2. It's display-styled body text — use a <p> with a class.
sub_considerations.body:
A heading announces a section. The lead doesn't start a section, it sits inside the article's implicit "intro". Marking it H2 splits the outline and confuses screen reader navigation.

If you want a sub-headline above the lead, use <hgroup>:

<hgroup>
  <h1>Variable fonts: an introduction</h1>
  <p class="lead">One file, every weight, every width…</p>
</hgroup>

sub_considerations.one_liner: Attribute to a named human. Generic "Editorial team" bylines hurt trust signals and EEAT.
sub_considerations.body: EEAT (Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, Trustworthiness) weighs author identity. Link the byline to a profile page with credentials. AI-assisted articles should still carry the human editor's byline; disclose the assistance in the body.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Show both Published and Updated when they differ — and explain in the body what changed.
sub_considerations.body: Stealth-republishing an old article with a fresh date is dishonest and Google penalises the pattern. Show both dates; if the update was substantive (>20% rewrite), add a brief changelog line at the bottom.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Wrap dates in <time datetime="…"> with ISO 8601 in the attribute.
sub_considerations.body:
The datetime attribute carries the unambiguous machine value; the element's text can be the prettified human form. Crawlers, calendars, and assistive tech all read the attribute.

<time datetime="2026-05-15T09:30:00+02:00">15 May 2026</time>

sub_considerations.one_liner: Reading-time estimates help, but use them honestly: 220–250 wpm for technical reading, not 300.
sub_considerations.body: The classic Medium formula assumed 265 wpm at average reading difficulty. Technical articles slow down; round up. Display as "~7 min read", not "6 min 42 s" — false precision suggests false confidence.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Cap measure at 60–75 characters per line. Wider needs leading; narrower hurts rhythm.
sub_considerations.body:
Use the ch unit and let the browser do the work:

.prose { max-width: 66ch; }

Beyond ~75 characters the eye loses the next line; below ~45 it scans more than it reads.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Base font size 16–18px. Anything smaller fails AA-level readability for many users.
sub_considerations.body: Use rem for body sizing so the user's browser zoom preference takes effect. WCAG SC 1.4.4 requires text to resize to 200% without loss of content; that fails if the layout is built in px with fixed containers.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Set body line-height between 1.5 and 1.7 (unitless). Tight leading is for headings.
sub_considerations.body: SC 1.4.12 (Text Spacing) requires the page to survive user-applied line-height of 1.5× body size. Building to that floor is the simplest path to compliance.

sub_considerations.one_liner: text-wrap: pretty is now Baseline newly available — use it on body prose for better orphans.
sub_considerations.body:
Browsers do a final-line balance pass to avoid single-word last lines. Cheap, no JS, no harm if unsupported.

.prose p { text-wrap: pretty; }
.prose h1, .prose h2, .prose h3 { text-wrap: balance; }

sub_considerations.one_liner: Enable hyphenation only with a language attribute on <html>, and only with a justified or narrow column.
sub_considerations.body: Hyphenation rules are language-specific. Without lang="en" the browser falls back to nothing or to the wrong dictionary, producing ugly breaks. At ragged-right widths above ~60ch hyphenation usually loses; turn it on for narrow columns only.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Always set width and height attributes on <img>. Eliminates CLS.
sub_considerations.body:
The attributes give the browser an aspect ratio to reserve space before bytes arrive. Setting height: auto in CSS preserves responsive scaling. CLS often drops by an order of magnitude after this single change.

<img src="hero.jpg" width="1600" height="900" alt="…" />

sub_considerations.one_liner: Use loading="lazy" for below-the-fold images. Never on the hero — it delays LCP.
sub_considerations.body: Pair loading="lazy" with decoding="async" to free up the main thread. For the LCP candidate image, do the opposite: fetchpriority="high", loading="eager", preconnect to the image origin.
sub_considerations.source_title: Use lazy-loading to improve loading speed

sub_considerations.one_liner: Alt text describes function, not appearance. A photo of a developer at a laptop is "a developer pair-programming", not "a man at a computer".
sub_considerations.body: The test: read the article aloud with the image replaced by its alt text. Does it make sense? Decorative images that add nothing to the prose take alt="" (not missing — empty), removing them from the accessibility tree.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Captioned media goes in <figure>/<figcaption>. Captions are not alt text; they complement it.
sub_considerations.body: Alt text describes what the image is; the caption adds editorial context, attribution, or interpretation. They can repeat, but should not duplicate verbatim.
sub_considerations.source_title: <figure>: the figure with optional caption element

sub_considerations.one_liner: Link the words that describe the destination. Never "click here", "read more", "this".
sub_considerations.body: Screen readers can list every link on the page in isolation. "Click here" eleven times is unusable. The link text alone should answer "where does this go?"

sub_considerations.one_liner: Underline inline links. Colour alone is not a sufficient signal (WCAG 1.4.1).
sub_considerations.body:
Use text-underline-offset and text-decoration-thickness to dial in an underline that respects descenders without looking heavy.

a {
  text-decoration-thickness: 1px;
  text-underline-offset: 2px;
}

sub_considerations.one_liner: Don't force target="_blank" for editorial links. Users own that decision; respect them.
sub_considerations.body:
The middle-click and cmd-click conventions exist; they're discoverable; users use them. Forcing a new tab breaks the back button, surprises keyboard users, and bypasses the user's preference. Reserve target="_blank" for true tool-style escape hatches (PDF previews, embedded forms).

When you do use it, pair with rel="noopener" to close the window-reference vector. Modern browsers default this; setting it explicitly is harmless.
sub_considerations.source_title: Opening links in new browser windows

sub_considerations.one_liner: Pull quotes belong in <aside>, not in <blockquote>. They're an editorial flourish, not a citation.
sub_considerations.body: A pull quote duplicates body content for emphasis. A blockquote cites external content. Confusing the two creates duplicate text for screen readers; <aside> with aria-hidden="true" on the pull quote is the cleanest pattern.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Use <blockquote cite="…"> for actual quotations; pair with a <cite> element for attribution.
sub_considerations.body:
<blockquote cite="https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/">
  <p>Provide ways to help users navigate…</p>
  <footer>— <cite>WCAG 2.2</cite></footer>
</blockquote>

The cite attribute carries a URL machines can parse; the <cite> element carries the human-readable source name.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Editorial callouts ("Tip", "Note", "Warning") don't need ARIA role="alert" — they're not interactive.
sub_considerations.body: ARIA role="alert" is for transient, dynamic messages screen readers should interrupt for. Static editorial callouts are body content with a visual treatment; mark them up as <aside> or <div> with a clear textual label ("Note:").

sub_considerations.one_liner: Editor-curated "related" beats algorithmic. Recency and topical-affinity feeds bury the best content.
sub_considerations.body: "Most recent" is a recency filter, not a relevance filter. For a 1,200-article archive, automated relatedness produces predictable misses. The cheapest fix is two manually-picked links per article in the CMS.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Show 3–5 related items. Beyond five and the user disengages; below three feels token.
sub_considerations.body: Baymard's editorial-site testing puts the engagement sweet spot at four. Below three the section looks unfinished; above five it becomes a list, which gets skimmed and skipped.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Each related item needs context: a one-sentence "why this is here", not a bare title.
sub_considerations.body: Bare titles ask the reader to evaluate "is this worth my time?" with too little signal. A line of context is editorial work that pays for itself in click-through.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Prefer navigator.share() on mobile; fall back to a copy-link button.
sub_considerations.body:
The Web Share API hands off to the user's native share sheet — every app they use, ordered by their habits. It's better than any custom button row.

Feature-detect:

if ('share' in navigator) {
  // show share button
} else {
  // show copy-link button
}

sub_considerations.one_liner: A wall of platform-specific share buttons usually hurts performance more than it helps reach.
sub_considerations.body: Most third-party share widgets ship tracking, fonts, and 100kB+ of JS. Measure click-through before optimising visual prominence; you'll likely find a copy-link button outperforms a six-button strip.
sub_considerations.source_title: The hidden cost of share buttons

sub_considerations.one_liner: Confirm "link copied" with a toast or in-place text change. Silent success feels broken.
sub_considerations.body: For screen reader users, fire an aria-live="polite" region with "Link copied" so the action announces. Visual confirmation alone fails non-sighted users.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Aim for ~150–160 characters. Google routinely truncates beyond ~158, and longer values get rewritten.
sub_considerations.body:
Google selects the snippet at query time. If your description is too long, irrelevant to the query, or absent, Google generates one from the page body. Write it for humans; the bot will use it if it matches intent.

On mobile. Snippets render shorter — front-load the first 60 characters with the answer.

On desktop. You get the full ~158 characters in most layouts.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Don't stuff keywords into the meta description. It's a snippet, not a ranking factor.
sub_considerations.body: Meta descriptions haven't been a ranking signal since 2009. Their only job is to win the click. Write them like the back of a book.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Every page gets a unique description. Duplicates trigger "duplicate meta description" warnings in Search Console.
sub_considerations.body: If you can't write a unique one, omit it and let Google generate. An auto-generated snippet from real body content beats a templated one that lies.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Required Article fields for Google: headline, image, datePublished, author.
sub_considerations.body:
Google's Article rich result requires these four; everything else (publisher, dateModified, description) is recommended. Validate before deploying with Google's Rich Results Test.

<script type="application/ld+json">
{
  "@context": "https://schema.org",
  "@type": "Article",
  "headline": "Variable fonts: an introduction",
  "image": "https://example.com/hero.jpg",
  "datePublished": "2026-05-15",
  "author": { "@type": "Person", "name": "J. Riley" }
}
</script>

sub_considerations.one_liner: Use JSON-LD, not microdata or RDFa. Google has preferred it since 2017.
sub_considerations.body: JSON-LD lives in a single <script type="application/ld+json"> tag, separate from your markup. It survives template refactors. Microdata couples structured data to specific tags and tends to drift out of sync.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Pick the most specific type: NewsArticle, BlogPosting, TechArticle — not generic Article.
sub_considerations.body: Subtypes inherit Article's fields and add their own. NewsArticle unlocks the Top Stories carousel; TechArticle hints at documentation context for AI summarisation. Picking the right one is a small lift with disproportionate effect.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Author must be a Person with at minimum name; add url to a stable author page.
sub_considerations.body: Google's 2024 update to the Article guidelines hardened the author requirement: it should be a Person (not Organization) with a name, ideally a stable URL the crawler can revisit. EEAT signals depend on this.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Four required OG fields: og:title, og:type, og:url, og:image.
sub_considerations.body: For articles, og:type is article. Add og:description for the preview snippet. Most LLM-based answer engines (Perplexity, ChatGPT search, Claude) read OG as the canonical title/description; treat it as first-class metadata.

sub_considerations.one_liner: og:image at 1200×630px, under 5MB, in PNG or JPEG. Text on the image should be ≥40px.
sub_considerations.body:
1200×630 is the safe aspect ratio across LinkedIn, Slack, Facebook, X, Discord. Some platforms crop to square; keep the focal subject inside the centre 630×630 region.

Auto-generate per-article OG images server-side. The investment pays back across years of shares.

sub_considerations.one_liner: For X / Twitter, set twitter:card to summary_large_image. Don't duplicate fields OG already covers.
sub_considerations.body: X falls back to the corresponding OG tag for any Twitter field you omit (image, title, description). The only Twitter-specific tags worth setting are twitter:card and twitter:site.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Body text contrast ≥ 4.5:1 (AA), ≥ 7:1 (AAA). Large text ≥ 3:1.
sub_considerations.body: WCAG 2.2 still uses the relative-luminance formula; WCAG 3 (draft) will replace it with APCA. For now design to the established floor and validate with a tool that reports both, so you're not surprised when WCAG 3 ships.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Non-text contrast ≥ 3:1. Catches: form borders, focus rings, chart strokes, icon-only buttons.
sub_considerations.body: This rule retroactively makes the popular pale-grey-on-white input border illegal. If a control's edge is the only thing distinguishing it from the page, it needs the 3:1 floor.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Tab order follows visual reading order. Don't use positive tabindex; rearrange the DOM instead.
sub_considerations.body: Positive tabindex overrides natural order and tends to drift out of sync with future edits. tabindex="0" and tabindex="-1" stay useful.

sub_considerations.one_liner: Use :focus-visible, not :focus. Mouse clicks shouldn't paint the focus ring.
sub_considerations.body:
Baseline-supported since 2022. The browser decides when focus is "keyboard-style" and paints the ring only then. Pair with :focus for older browsers if needed.

button:focus-visible {
  outline: 2px solid var(--blue-9);
  outline-offset: 2px;
}
